channel.title: Pratik Dhanave — Blog
channel.description: Long-form writing on multi-agent AI, medical AI governance, HIPAA-aware architecture, and cloud-native systems.

item.title: Lessons from Converting 18 Agents in 90 Days
item.description: What worked, what was hard, and what we'd do differently. Real numbers: 18 agents, 90 days, 5 governance policies, 4 provider swaps.

item.title: Deployment & A2A: From ADK's Web Deploy to MAF's Scalable Architecture
item.description: Cloud Run deployments, agent-to-agent communication, load balancing, and production observability.

item.title: Callbacks and Middleware: Porting ADK Hooks to MAF's Decorator Pattern
item.description: Callbacks to middleware: composable decorators for audit, retry, token enforcement, and OpenTelemetry integration.

item.title: Provider Abstraction: From Gemini-Only to Swappable LLMs
item.description: Zero-code provider swaps: Ollama (dev), OpenAI (staging), Azure Foundry (prod). Same agents, different models.

item.title: Tool Wrapping: From ADK Functions to MAF Governed Tools
item.description: From ADK functions to MAF governed tools. Adding policy enforcement, DLP, approval gates, and OPA integration.

item.title: Token Exchange Patterns: Porting Multi-Turn State from ADK to MAF
item.description: Sessions to threads: porting multi-turn state from ADK to MAF. Token budgeting, long-term memory, and conversation audit trails.

item.title: The Executor Pattern: ADK→MAF Conversion for Agentic Control Flow
item.description: How to port ADK's orchestration callbacks to MAF builders without losing control. The executor pattern: you own the loop.

item.title: Why We Migrated from Google ADK to Microsoft MARA
item.description: The philosophy, trade-offs, and what we learned converting 18+ agents in 3 months. Provider abstraction as the foundation for portable agents.

item.title: OpenTelemetry and Evaluation in Multi-Agent Workflows — the full production stack
item.description: How to instrument multi-agent systems with OpenTelemetry, propagate trace context across an in-memory bus, and build a layered evaluation pipeline — from real-time policy gates to async LLM-as-judge to SLO-based trust scoring. Everything I learned building Genie.

item.title: Running a HIPAA-Aware Multi-Agent Medical AI on GKE: A Field Map
item.description: Google's GKE AI infrastructure docs list ~40 integrations. Here's a field map of which ones actually matter when the workload is a HIPAA-aware multi-agent medical AI, and where the gaps sit.

item.title: The Right-to-Explanation Handler: GDPR Article 22 as a Go HTTP Endpoint
item.description: How a 200-line Go handler turns an audit log and an eval store into a regulator-friendly answer to 'why did the AI decide that?'

item.title: Time-bound privileged access — building the PCSE §1.4 PAM analog in Go
item.description: Request → N-eyes approve → window-of-time → automatic expiry, with every transition written to a hash-chained audit log. The package that closes Gap #1 from the PCSE map.

item.title: HL7 v2 in 2026: Why This 50-Year-Old Protocol Still Drives Healthcare Integration
item.description: Why HL7 v2 — a 50-year-old pipe-delimited protocol — still drives most US hospital ADT integrations in 2026, and what a clean Go parser looks like in ~300 lines.

item.title: Studying for the AIGP? Here's a Reference Implementation in Go
item.description: Studying for the IAPP AI Governance Professional credential? Here's an open-source Go codebase that demonstrates ~70% of the body of knowledge in working code.

item.title: "Enough to Reconstruct, Never Enough to Leak": The HIPAA Audit Log Design Problem
item.description: Enough to reconstruct, never enough to leak. The audit event schema problem under §164.312(b), and how to solve it without conflating the audit sink with the PHI sink.

item.title: The Fallback Is the Contract: Reliability Patterns for Clinical AI
item.description: Every LLM-backed agent in this platform has a deterministic rule-based fallback. The case always finalises. The fallback isn't a workaround — it's the contract.

item.title: Multi-Agent Systems in 5 Interfaces: A MARA Implementation Field Report
item.description: Five interfaces hold the whole platform together. The 30-line orchestrator closure that makes the rest of the architecture testable, auditable, and safe to evolve.

item.title: PostgreSQL Row-Level Security Is HIPAA Defense in Depth
item.description: PostgreSQL row-level security as HIPAA defence in depth. Why fail-open application filtering isn't enough, and how 'append-only at DB GRANTs' carries more of the §164.312(b) burden than people realise.

item.title: The 21st Century Cures Act, Expressed in Go
item.description: The 21st Century Cures Act §3060 CDS carve-out criterion 4 expressed as a code-level queue, lossless on reject, with audit-recorded reviewer rationale. Build it once, satisfy GDPR Article 22 for free.

item.title: Moving Diagnostic Accuracy 42.9% → 85.7% by Changing Two Files
item.description: How a single sprint of specialty-rule work — guided by a benchmark that wasn't afraid to print embarrassing numbers — turned a 'demo respiratory differential' into a five-condition rule-based diagnostic engine.

item.title: Building a HIPAA-Aware Medical AI Platform in Go: An Architecture & Compliance Deep Dive
item.description: What HIPAA looks like when you express it as Go interfaces — governance policies, append-only audit at DB GRANTs, PHI redaction at the logger seam, and HITL as the §3060 CDS carve-out criterion 4.

item.title: The MERGE pattern that cost ten times more than INSERT-then-UPDATE — a ₹100 Cr lesson
item.description: What looked like an idiomatic BigQuery MERGE was scanning the full target table on every batch. The fix was syntactic, not architectural — and it was the single biggest contributor to a 57% data-warehouse cost reduction across the Tata Group engagement.

item.title: The 57% number — how we cut the Tata Group BigQuery bill in half
item.description: ₹100 Cr / ~$12M in proven savings across a year-plus engagement. The four levers that did the heavy lifting, the lever I expected to win that didn't, and the post-engagement playbook that became a Searce managed service.

item.title: Optimus — a Gemini-powered BigQuery anti-pattern detector that paid for itself in a week
item.description: We built a small Go + Python service that parses a project's INFORMATION_SCHEMA, asks Gemini to classify each top-spending query against a catalog of anti-patterns, and recommends a rewrite. It is not a magic box; it is a pipeline that cuts the human review time per query from 20 minutes to 90 seconds.

item.title: BigQuery slot reservation transitions — when to commit, when to stay on-demand
item.description: Capacity-based slot reservation is the biggest single FinOps lever for predictable batch workloads, but the transition is harder than the math. Notes from sizing reservations across enterprise GCP customers.

item.title: BigQuery storage tiering — physical bytes, logical bytes, and the savings hiding in your old partitions
item.description: Storage was the second-biggest line item on the Tata BigQuery bill. Long-term storage, physical-vs-logical billing, and column-level retention together took a 6-figure monthly line down to a 5-figure one.

item.title: The Spanner Migration Tool — a contributor's reading map
item.description: Notes from contributing to Google's open-source Spanner Migration Tool (HarbourBridge). Where to start reading the codebase, where the load-bearing logic lives, and the parts that look simple but aren't.

item.title: Primary-key design for Cloud Spanner — preventing write hotspots, 40-60% performance gains
item.description: Spanner partitions by primary-key range. A monotonically-increasing PK like a timestamp or UUID-v1 funnels all writes to one server. The fix changes everything from your sequence strategy to your tenant model.

item.title: Spanner interleaved tables — when and when not
item.description: Interleaving a child table into its parent co-locates the rows for fast joins. It also tightens coupling in ways that bite you on the next schema migration. A practitioner's decision matrix.

item.title: CDC for minimal-downtime Spanner migration — Datastream + Pub/Sub + Dataflow
item.description: A bulk migration takes hours; the application can't be offline that long. CDC keeps the source and destination in sync while the bulk runs, and a quick cutover swaps traffic. The handoff between bulk and CDC is where most migrations go wrong.

item.title: Bloom — Terraform for regulated bank cloud provisioning, SOC 2 and ISO 27001 from day one
item.description: Notes from contributing to Bloom — SC Ventures / Standard Chartered's policy-driven secure cloud provisioning platform. Push-to-deploy self-service for bank engineering teams, with the audit controls baked in.

item.title: SOC 2 controls as Terraform modules — turning the audit from a project into a daily build
item.description: If you encode each SOC 2 control as a Terraform module, the audit becomes a check against module usage rather than a per-resource review. Notes from Bloom and adjacent projects.

item.title: airshipit + OpenTelemetry — 30% reduction in manual ops in a multi-vendor OSS project
item.description: Notes from integrating OpenTelemetry into airshipit, an open-source bare-metal Kubernetes lifecycle project with contributions from Ericsson, AT&T, Microsoft, and others. The hard part wasn't OTel; it was making distributed traces useful across foreign code.

item.title: azure-service-operator — collaborating with Ericsson, AT&T, and Microsoft on a Kubernetes-native Azure controller
item.description: The azure-service-operator project lets you declare Azure resources as Kubernetes objects. Notes from the multi-vendor collaboration shape: how decisions got made, what slowed us down, what shipped despite it.

item.title: Picnic — cutting API latency 47% by consolidating microservices behind protobuf contracts
item.description: The Picnic social platform served 1M+ users across a graph of Go microservices behind a GraphQL gateway. The latency win came from a counter-intuitive move: fewer services, tighter contracts.

item.title: Picnic — 80%+ test coverage and Prometheus observability turned days-to-detect into minutes-to-detect
item.description: Test coverage and observability are the boring infrastructure that makes the interesting changes safe. Notes on how the Picnic team built both, and the on-call experience they enabled.

item.title: Globe — running a 30K+ TPS transaction platform on Kubernetes
item.description: The transaction engine had to absorb 30K+ TPS across partner integrations, never lose a transaction, and survive partial failures. The architecture: Go, Kafka, Pub/Sub, Redis, K8s, with idempotency at every layer.

item.title: Idempotency at three layers — the pattern that kept the Globe transaction engine honest
item.description: A single layer of idempotency will eventually fail. Three independent layers gives you a margin. Here is the pattern that worked across ingest, worker, and emit boundaries.

item.title: Error-code orchestration — replacing a giant switch statement with a typed enum across partner adapters
item.description: Status-code-based dispatch made every worker grow a longer and longer switch. Normalising every partner-specific error into an enumerated set let the orchestration logic stop changing as new partners landed.

item.title: Double-entry ledger invariants — building a P2P lending platform that never loses money
item.description: 5K+ loans per month. Three credit bureaus. Multiple payment gateways. The thing that has to be right is the ledger. Notes on what invariants the database enforces vs what the application enforces.

item.title: P2P lending — KYC/AML, three credit bureaus, and the maker-checker RBAC governance that kept fraud rates low
item.description: Borrower onboarding is the most fraud-prone moment in a P2P platform. The shape that worked: deterministic KYC, parallel bureau pulls with fallback, real-time fraud signals, and a maker-checker approval for every disbursement.

item.title: Brownlow — zero-trust voting on Cloud Run during live AFL broadcasts
item.description: 100K+ votes, 10K+ concurrent users during a live AFL Brownlow Medal broadcast. The architecture: Go on Cloud Run, GraphQL + gRPC behind a CDN, vote integrity through Cloud KMS + Security Command Center. Notes on what makes a live-broadcast load shape unusual.

item.title: Speaking at Google Cloud Next 2022 — the monolith-to-microservices talk
item.description: 30 minutes on stage. The talk title looked tactical; the talk underneath was about why most microservices migrations fail and how to set up the one that doesn't.

item.title: Google Summer of Code mentor 2019-2026 — what I learned guiding 10+ students through open-source projects
item.description: Seven cycles. Ten-plus students. Most shipped, a few didn't, all of them taught me something about engineering culture. Notes on what works for mentors and what works for students.

item.title: Gocloud — designing a unified API library for AWS, GCP, and Azure
item.description: What it actually takes to build a unified cloud API library — and why "write once, run anywhere" still doesn't quite work, even for the patterns where it almost does.

item.title: Mapping a multi-agent platform to the GCP PCSE blueprint
item.description: Every Professional Cloud Security Engineer exam bullet, mapped to a file path in an RBI FREE-AI aligned Go platform. Where the implementation matches, where the analog substitutes, and where the honest gaps are.

item.title: Defence in depth for agentic AI — the eleven-layer envelope
item.description: The mental model that says no two adjacent layers share a single point of failure for the same class of attack. From TLS to OTel, the eleven layers a customer request crosses before an answer comes back.

item.title: Consolidated security deep-dive — RBAC, RLS, token exchange, encryption, audit
item.description: The long-form security narrative for a multi-agent financial assistant — authentication, authorisation, tenant isolation, dual-identity audit, envelope encryption, hash-chained logs, governance, red team, BCP.

item.title: AI governance — from credential to codebase
item.description: Board policy as a YAML file the risk team owns. Annexure VI as a database query. Every governance recommendation rendered as a file path in a Go repository.

item.title: Agentic security in production — the operations playbook
item.description: Twelve months of running multi-agent AI in a regulated context. SLIs that matter, the incident runbook, drift detection, continuous adversarial testing, secret rotation, compliance posture as code.
